full_notes:
Friction is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that sOME NATURAL PHENOMENA SOME NATURAL PHENOMENA In this chapter we shall discuss two destructive natural phenomena.

The text explains that we shall also discuss what steps we can take to minimise destruction caused by these phenomena.

The text explains that 12.1 Lightning You might have seen sparks on a electric pole when wires become loose.

The text explains that this phenomenon is quite common when wind is blowing and shaking the wires.

The text explains that you might also have seen sparks when a plug is loose in its socket.

The text explains that lightning is also an electric spark, but on a huge scale.

The text explains that in ancient times, people did not understand the cause of these sparks.

The text explains that they were, therefore, afraid of lightning and thought that the wrath of gods was visiting them.

The text explains that now, of course, we understand that lightning is caused by the accumulation of charges in the clouds.

The text explains that we need not be afraid of lightning, but we have to take precautions to protect ourselves from the deadly sparks.

The text explains that the Sparks that the Greeks Knew About The ancient Greeks knew as early as 600 B.C. that when amber (amber is a kind of resin) was rubbed with fur, it attracted light objects such as hair.

The text explains that you might have seen that when you take off woollen or polyester clothes, your hair stands on end.

The text explains that if you take off these clothes in the dark, you even see a spark and hear a crackling sound.

The text explains that in 1752 Benjamin Franklin, an American scientist, showed that lightning and the spark from your clothes are essentially the same phenomena.

The text explains that however, it took 2000 years for this realisation to occur.

The text explains that we shall now study some properties of electric charges.

The text explains that in the process of charging the refill and the plastic comb, polythene and hair also get charged.

The text explains that can you explain why the foil strips repel each other?

The text explains that the reason is that the foil strips lose charge to the earth through your body.

The text explains that the process of transferring of charge from a charged object to the earth is called earthing.

The text explains that earthing is provided in buildings to protect us from electrical shocks due to any leakage of electrical current.

The text explains that 12.5 The Story of Lightning It is now possible to explain lightning in terms of the charges produced by rubbing.

The text explains that by a process, not yet completely understood, the positive charges collect near the upper edges of the clouds and the negative charges accumulate near the lower edges.

The text explains that negative and positive charges meet, producing streaks of bright light and sound.

The text explains that sOME NATURAL PHENOMENA 155 The process of electric discharge can occur between two or more clouds, or between clouds and the earth.

The text explains that today we need not get frightened by lightning like our ancestors did.

The text explains that 12.7 Earthquakes You just learnt about thunderstorm and lightning.

The text explains that a major earthquake occurred in India on 8 October 2005 in Uri and Tangdhar towns of North Kashmir (Fig.

The text explains that before that a major earthquake occurred on 26 January 2001 in Bhuj district of Gujarat.

The text explains that collect a few pictures showing the damage caused by these earthquakes from newspapers and magazines of those days.

The text explains that prepare a short report on the suffering of the people during and after the earthquakes.

The text explains that these are some of the questions which we shall discuss below.

The text explains that an earthquake is a sudden shaking or trembling of the earth which lasts for a very short time.

The text explains that it is caused by a disturbance deep inside the earth’s Fig.

The text explains that sCIENCE 158 My grandmother told me that the earth is balanced on the horn of a bull and when the bull shifts it to the other horn, an earthquake takes place.

The text explains that earthquakes occur all the time, all over the earth.

The text explains that earthquakes can cause floods, landslides and tsunamis.

The text explains that 12.10 : Earth plates What could cause a disturbance inside the earth?

The text explains that 12.9 : Structure of the earth Now we know that the tremors are caused by the disturbance deep down inside the uppermost layer of the earth called the crust (Fig.

The text explains that in ancient times, people did not know the true cause of earthquakes.

The text explains that the outermost layer of the earth is not in one piece.

The text explains that when they brush past one another, Arabian Plate Eurasian Plate Indan Plate crust mantle outer core inner core 2024-25

The text explains that 12.11), they cause disturbance in the earth’s crust.

The text explains that it is this disturbance that shows up as an earthquake on the surface of the earth. carried out.

The text explains that however, most earthquakes are caused by the movement of earth’s plates.

The text explains that since earthquakes are caused by the movement of plates, the boundaries of the plates are the weak zones where earthquakes are more likely to occur.

The text explains that if scientists know so much about earthquakes, can they also predict when and where the next one will strike?

The text explains that 12.12 : Movements of Indian earth’s plate The power of an earthquake is expressed in terms of a magnitude on a scale called the Richter scale.

The text explains that really destructive earthquakes have magnitudes higher than 7 on the Richter scale.

The text explains that both Bhuj and Kashmir earthquakes had magnitudes greater than 7.5.

The text explains that although, we know for sure what causes an earthquake, it is not yet possible to predict when and where the next earthquake might occur.

The text explains that go to a room where there is a fluorescent tube light.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Science, Chapter 12: Friction
